draft.introduction: Saadi’s Bustan (The Orchard, 1257 CE) is a didactic poem in ten chapters covering justice, generosity, love, humility, and contentment. Where the Gulistan mixes prose and poetry, the Bustan is entirely in verse.

translation.text: In the name of him who created the soul and adorned it with thought, who illumined the inward eye and quickened the tongue with speech. He gives sustenance without hand, and foot, and without eye beholds the hidden and the manifest. He is the first, before whom there was no first; he is the last, beyond whom there is no beyond.
translation.original_text: به نام خدایی که جان آفرید / سخن گفتن اندر زبان آفرید

translator_notes.note: Saadi’s invocation parallels Quranic attributes of God (the First and the Last, al-Awwal and al-Akhir, Quran 57:3). The poetic theology emphasizes God’s transcendence of sensory categories (“without hand, foot, or eye”) while affirming intimate divine activity in creation—a balance central to Islamic theology between tanzih (transcendence) and tashbih (immanence).
